question: The parietal and temporal bones of the skull are joined by a ________
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Identify types and examples of fibrous joints.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Sutures are immovable fibrous joints found exclusively between flat bones of the skull.

question: Which of the following is NOT a synovial joint?
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: The symphysis pubis is a cartilaginous joint containing a fibrocartilaginous pad, not a synovial joint.

question: Bones of a synovial joint are held together by ________
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Describe structural components of a synovial joint.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: The articular joint capsule encloses the joint cavity, holding articulating bones together.

question: Which of the following movements could occur at the hip joint?
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Identify angular and rotational joint movements.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: As a multiaxial ball-and-socket joint, the hip joint permits flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, circumduction, and rotation.

question: Which of the following allows the most possible movements?
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Classify types of synovial joints by range of motion.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Ball-and-socket joints offer multiaxial movement, granting the greatest freedom of motion of all joint types.

question: What is the movement called that brings the foot closer to the shin?
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Describe special movements of the foot and ankle.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Dorsiflexion bends the foot superiorly at the ankle joint towards the shin.

question: When you turn your head to look over your shoulder that is an example of
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Identify rotational movements of the head and spine.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Turning the head side-to-side is cervical rotation occurring between the atlas and axis vertebrae.

question: Joints that are functionally classified as freely movable are termed
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Compare functional classifications of joints: synarthrotic, amphiarthrotic, diarthrotic.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Diarthrotic joints are functionally defined as freely movable joints, comprising all synovial joints.

question: Intervertebral discs between adjacent vertebrae are classified structurally as which type of cartilaginous joint?
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Identify types and structural characteristics of cartilaginous joints.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: A symphysis is a cartilaginous joint where bone ends are connected by a resilient pad of fibrocartilage.

question: Which tissue covers the articulating bone ends within a synovial joint to reduce friction and absorb shock?
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Describe functions of articular cartilage in synovial joints.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: A smooth layer of articular hyaline cartilage covers bone epiphyses in synovial joints to minimize friction during joint movement.

question: Fluid-filled sacs lined with synovial membrane that cushion tendons passing over bony prominences are called
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Describe accessory synovial structures including bursae and tendon sheaths.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Bursae are fluid-filled sacs that reduce friction where tendons, ligaments, or muscle rub against bones.

question: The elbow joint (between the humerus and ulna) is an example of which type of synovial joint?
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Identify examples and movements of hinge joints.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Hinge joints permit uniaxial movement in one plane, allowing flexion and extension at the elbow and knee.

question: Moving a limb away from the midline of the body along the frontal plane is defined as
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Define angular movements including abduction and adduction.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Abduction is the angular movement of a body part laterally away from the midline.

question: Turning the hand so that the palm faces anteriorly (or upward in anatomical position) is called
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Distinguish between supination and pronation of the forearm.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Supination rotates the forearm laterally so the palm faces anteriorly (or upward).

question: Turning the sole of the foot laterally outward is known as
subject: E - Skeletal System & Joints
sloDescription: Describe special foot movements: inversion and eversion.
chapter: Chapter 8: Joints of the Skeletal System
explanation: Eversion turns the sole of the foot laterally outward away from the body midline.
